name: Pakistan
tagline: From the planned cities of the Indus to a nuclear-armed republic.
summary: Pakistan occupies one of the oldest cradles of civilisation on Earth, where the Bronze Age cities of Mohenjo-daro and Harappa flourished more than four thousand years ago along the Indus. Over the millennia its valleys and passes carried Persians, Greeks, Buddhists of Gandhara, Muslim sultans and the Mughal emperors who made Lahore a jewel of their realm. In the twentieth century the demand of British India's Muslims for a homeland — crystallised in the 1940 Lahore Resolution under Muhammad Ali Jinnah — produced the modern state, which won independence on 14 August 1947 amid the trauma of Partition. Since then Pakistan has lived through the loss of its eastern wing in 1971, recurring cycles of civilian and military rule, and its emergence as a nuclear power and the world's fifth most populous nation.

founding.label: Independence from Britain
founding.detail: Pakistan came into being at midnight on 14 August 1947 under the Indian Independence Act, which partitioned British India into two dominions. Muhammad Ali Jinnah, revered as Quaid-e-Azam, took office as the country's first Governor-General, and Liaquat Ali Khan became its first prime minister. The new state — then comprising West Pakistan and a distant East Pakistan separated by some 1,600 km of Indian territory — was born amid one of the largest forced migrations in history.

quickFacts.label: UNESCO sites
quickFacts.value: Mohenjo-daro, Taxila, Lahore Fort, Makli, Rohtas

eras.title: The Indus Valley Civilisation
eras.standfirst: Along the Indus and its tributaries arose one of the world's earliest urban civilisations, its grid-planned cities a wonder of the Bronze Age.
body: The Indus, or Harappan, civilisation was a Bronze Age society whose two great centres — Mohenjo-daro in Sindh and Harappa in Punjab, some 640 km apart — both lie within modern Pakistan. Its mature urban phase is usually dated to roughly 2600–1900 BCE, contemporary with ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia. The civilisation consisted of these two large cities and more than a hundred towns and villages spread across the north-western subcontinent.

The cities astonish for their order. Mohenjo-daro, home to perhaps 35,000 people, was laid out on a grid of baked-brick houses with well-ordered wastewater drainage, covered sewers, public wells and a monumental Great Bath atop its citadel. Standardised weights and measures and a uniform material culture across hundreds of settlements point to extensive trade and shared administration. The Harappans worked copper and bronze, fired distinctive seals, and traded as far as Mesopotamia.

They left a script of short inscriptions that has never been deciphered, so their language, beliefs and rulers remain unknown. From around 1900 BCE the cities were gradually abandoned, a decline now linked chiefly to climate change and weakening monsoon-fed rivers rather than any single invasion. Rediscovered by archaeologists in 1921–22, Mohenjo-daro was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980, the earliest chapter of Pakistan's recorded past.

events.title: Mature phase of the Indus cities
events.summary: Mohenjo-daro, Harappa and hundreds of other settlements reach their peak, with grid-planned streets, advanced drainage and standardised weights across a vast trading network.

events.title: Mohenjo-daro flourishes
events.summary: On the right bank of the Indus in Sindh, Mohenjo-daro becomes one of the civilisation's largest cities, renowned for its citadel and Great Bath; it is today a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: Decline of the Harappan cities
events.summary: The major cities are gradually abandoned as weakening monsoons and shifting rivers undermine the agricultural base, ending the civilisation's urban phase.

pullquote.text: One of the two main centres of the ancient Indus civilization, the other being Harappa.

eras.title: Gandhara and the Ancient Empires
eras.standfirst: Persians, Greeks, Mauryans and Kushans swept through the Indus passes, and at Taxila and Peshawar the Gandhara school fused Greece and Buddhism in stone.
body: From around 518 BCE the Achaemenid Persian kings drew the Indus lands into their empire as among its richest and most populous provinces, introducing centralised administration and Aramaic script. In 326 BCE Alexander the Great crossed the passes and defeated the local king Porus at the Battle of the Hydaspes on the Jhelum, carrying his army to the easternmost edge of his conquests before turning back. His departure left a Hellenistic imprint on the region's art and cities.

Soon after, Chandragupta Maurya absorbed the Indus territories into the first great Indian empire, and under his grandson Ashoka in the third century BCE the region embraced Buddhism. The area's centre of gravity was Gandhara, the kingdom around modern Peshawar (Purushapura) and Taxila (Takshashila), whose famed centre of learning drew scholars for a thousand years and is today a UNESCO World Heritage Site illustrating successive Persian, Greek and Central Asian influences.

Gandhara reached its zenith under the Kushans, a Central Asian dynasty whose greatest ruler, Kanishka, made Peshawar his capital in the early centuries CE. A patron of Buddhism, Kanishka promoted Mahayana teaching and convened a great Buddhist council. Under Kushan rule the Gandhara school of art flourished, depicting the Buddha in human form for the first time with the flowing drapery and classical features of Greco-Roman sculpture — a unique synthesis that radiated along the Silk Road into Central Asia and China.

events.title: Achaemenid Persia annexes the Indus lands
events.summary: The Persian empire incorporates the Indus region as among its wealthiest provinces, bringing centralised administration and Aramaic writing to the area.

events.title: Alexander defeats Porus at the Hydaspes
events.summary: Alexander the Great crosses the Indus passes and defeats King Porus on the Jhelum, marking the easternmost reach of his empire before his army turns homeward.

events.title: Kanishka and the Gandhara golden age
events.summary: Under the Kushan emperor Kanishka, ruling from Peshawar, Mahayana Buddhism and the Greco-Buddhist Gandhara school of art flourish, depicting the Buddha in human form.

figures.name: Kanishka
figures.blurb: The greatest Kushan ruler, who governed Gandhara from Peshawar, championed Mahayana Buddhism and convened a great Buddhist council. His reign saw the flowering of the Gandhara school of art.

eras.title: Islam, the Sultans and the Mughals
eras.standfirst: Islam arrived with Arab armies in Sindh, spread under the Delhi sultans, and reached its height under the Mughals, who made Lahore a city of imperial splendour.
body: Islam reached the subcontinent through the Indus when, in 711 CE, the young Umayyad general Muhammad ibn Qasim conquered Sindh and southern Punjab, planting the faith in what is now Pakistan. Over the following centuries waves of Turkic and Afghan conquerors followed the same north-western route. From 1206 the Delhi Sultanate, a Persianate Islamic state ruled in turn by five dynasties, dominated much of the region, deepening the Muslim character of the Indus plains and leaving an enduring imprint on language, law and architecture.

In 1526 Babur, a Central Asian prince descended from Timur and Genghis Khan, defeated the last Lodi sultan and founded the Mughal Empire, which fused Persian, Islamic and Indian culture into one of the wealthiest realms of its age. Lahore became a favoured imperial capital. Under Akbar, Jahangir and Shah Jahan the Mughals raised the Lahore Fort and the Shalimar Gardens — together a UNESCO World Heritage Site testifying to Mughal artistry at its height — while at Thatta in Sindh the necropolis of Makli grew into one of the largest in the world.

The synthesis reached its architectural climax under Aurangzeb, who between 1671 and 1673 built the vast Badshahi Mosque opposite the Lahore Fort, for centuries the largest mosque in the world. Yet his long wars in the Deccan, heavy taxation and religious friction strained the empire. After his death in 1707 Mughal authority fragmented rapidly, opening the Indus lands to regional powers — Sikhs in Punjab, the talpurs in Sindh — and ultimately to European intrusion.

events.title: Muhammad ibn Qasim conquers Sindh
events.summary: An Umayyad army under Muhammad ibn Qasim conquers Sindh and southern Punjab, bringing Islam to the Indus lands for the first time.

events.title: Rise of the Delhi Sultanate
events.summary: Muslim dynasties ruling from Delhi extend their dominion over the Indus plains, deepening the region's Islamic culture over three centuries.

events.title: Babur founds the Mughal Empire
events.summary: Babur's victory ends the Delhi Sultanate and establishes the Mughal Empire, which makes Lahore one of its great capitals.

events.title: Badshahi Mosque built at Lahore
events.summary: Emperor Aurangzeb commissions the monumental Badshahi Mosque opposite the Lahore Fort, for centuries the largest mosque in the world and a landmark of Mughal Lahore.

figures.name: Babur
figures.blurb: A Central Asian prince descended from Timur and Genghis Khan who founded the Mughal Empire in 1526, beginning a dynasty that would make Lahore one of its most splendid cities.

eras.title: The British Raj and the Pakistan Movement
eras.standfirst: British annexation of Punjab brought the Indus lands under the Raj; a century later, the demand for a Muslim homeland gathered behind Jinnah and the Lahore Resolution.
body: The British East India Company annexed Sindh in 1843 and the Sikh kingdom of Punjab in 1849, bringing the future Pakistan under colonial rule. After the rebellion of 1857 the Crown abolished Company rule and governed India directly as the British Raj. The Raj built canals, railways and a unified administration across the Indus plains, but its rule was extractive and its priorities imperial, and it left a region of immense diversity bound into a single colonial order.

From the late nineteenth century, India's Muslims grew anxious about their place in a future independent India dominated by a Hindu majority. The All-India Muslim League, founded in 1906, became the vehicle of their politics, and under Muhammad Ali Jinnah — a barrister once hailed as an apostle of Hindu-Muslim unity — it moved towards the conviction that Muslims formed a distinct nation. This 'two-nation theory' would become the ideological foundation of Pakistan.

The demand crystallised on 23 March 1940, when the Muslim League, meeting in Lahore, adopted the Lahore Resolution calling for independent states in the Muslim-majority regions of British India. Through the Second World War and its aftermath, Jinnah pressed the case for a separate homeland with growing success. When Britain finally resolved to quit India, the Indian Independence Act of July 1947 provided for the partition of the subcontinent and the creation of Pakistan.

events.title: Britain annexes the Punjab
events.summary: After defeating the Sikh kingdom, the British annex Punjab, completing colonial control over the lands that would become Pakistan.

events.title: Founding of the All-India Muslim League
events.summary: The All-India Muslim League is founded to represent the political interests of India's Muslims; it would become the engine of the Pakistan Movement.

events.title: The Lahore Resolution
events.summary: Meeting in Lahore, the Muslim League under Jinnah adopts the resolution demanding independent states for Muslims, the foundational charter of the Pakistan Movement.

events.title: Indian Independence Act passed
events.summary: The British Parliament passes the Indian Independence Act, partitioning British India and providing for the creation of the dominions of India and Pakistan.

figures.name: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
figures.blurb: A Karachi-born barrister and leader of the All-India Muslim League who championed the two-nation theory and led the movement that created Pakistan, becoming its first Governor-General. He is revered as Quaid-e-Azam, the 'Great Leader'.

pullquote.text: The founder and first governor-general (1947–48) of Pakistan… revered in Pakistan as Quaid-e-Azam.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopædia Britannica on Mohammed Ali Jinnah

eras.title: Partition and Independence
eras.standfirst: Freedom on 14 August 1947 came with the agony of Partition; a fractured early democracy gave way to military rule and, in 1971, the loss of the eastern wing.
body: At midnight on 14 August 1947 Pakistan became independent, with Jinnah as Governor-General and Liaquat Ali Khan as prime minister. The new state was a geographic paradox: West Pakistan and East Pakistan (formerly East Bengal) lay on either side of more than 1,600 km of Indian territory. Independence was inseparable from Partition, as a boundary commission under Cyril Radcliffe hastily divided Punjab and Bengal, splitting communities that had lived together for generations.

Partition triggered one of the largest and most violent forced migrations in history. Millions of Muslims, Hindus and Sikhs fled across the new borders in opposite directions; estimates of the dead range widely, commonly cited between several hundred thousand and around two million, amid massacres, mass abductions and sexual violence. The trauma, together with the unresolved status of the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir, ignited the first of several wars with India and has shadowed relations between the two states ever since.

The young state struggled to find its footing. Jinnah died in September 1948 and Liaquat Ali Khan was assassinated in 1951, leaving a vacuum of leadership. After years of constitutional instability, General Ayub Khan seized power in 1958 in the first of Pakistan's military coups, governing for a decade. Resentment in East Pakistan — more populous yet politically marginalised — deepened until, in 1971, it erupted into a war of secession that would redraw the map once more.

events.title: Independence of Pakistan
events.summary: Pakistan becomes independent under the Indian Independence Act, with Jinnah as Governor-General and Liaquat Ali Khan as prime minister, comprising West and East Pakistan.

events.title: Partition migration and violence
events.summary: The Radcliffe Line divides Punjab and Bengal, and millions are uprooted across the new borders amid massacres and mass migration, with deaths estimated from the hundreds of thousands into the millions.

events.title: Assassination of Liaquat Ali Khan
events.summary: Pakistan's first prime minister is assassinated at a rally in Rawalpindi, deepening the political instability of the new state's early years.

events.title: Ayub Khan's military coup
events.summary: General Ayub Khan seizes power in Pakistan's first military takeover, beginning a decade of presidential rule and a recurring pattern of army intervention in politics.

figures.name: Liaquat Ali Khan
figures.blurb: A close associate of Jinnah and a leading figure of the Muslim League, Liaquat Ali Khan became Pakistan's first prime minister in 1947 and guided the fledgling state until his assassination in 1951.

pullquote.text: At midnight at the end of 14 August 1947 the new dominion of Pakistan was born.
pullquote.attribution: On the Indian Independence Act of 1947

eras.title: Bangladesh, Military Rule and the Modern Republic
eras.standfirst: The loss of East Pakistan in 1971 remade the country, which has since cycled between civilian and military rule, become a nuclear power, and grown into a vast, youthful nation.
body: In 1971 East Pakistan rose against rule from the west. After the army launched Operation Searchlight in March, atrocities and a refugee exodus into India followed; India intervened militarily in December, and Pakistani forces in the east surrendered at Dhaka on 16 December, giving birth to Bangladesh. The defeat dismembered the country and discredited the military. Power passed to Zulfikar Ali Bhutto, who as president and then prime minister gave Pakistan a new 1973 constitution, nationalised industry and launched its nuclear programme.

In 1977 General Mohammad Zia-ul-Haq overthrew Bhutto, who was tried and hanged in 1979. Zia imposed martial law and a sweeping programme of Islamisation until his death in a plane crash in 1988. Democracy returned, alternating Benazir Bhutto and Nawaz Sharif, until General Pervez Musharraf seized power in 1999. In 1998 Pakistan had answered India's tests with nuclear detonations of its own in Balochistan, declaring itself a nuclear-armed state.

Civilian rule resumed after Musharraf, though the assassination of Benazir Bhutto on the eve of the 2008 elections underscored its fragility. In the years since, Pakistan has weathered insurgency and terrorism, devastating floods, and continued tension with India over Kashmir, while completing its first transfers of power between elected governments. Today it is the world's fifth most populous country — a young, populous, nuclear-armed republic still negotiating the balance between civilian authority and a powerful military.

events.title: Loss of East Pakistan and birth of Bangladesh
events.summary: After a brutal war and Indian intervention, Pakistani forces surrender at Dhaka and East Pakistan becomes the independent state of Bangladesh, dismembering the country.

events.title: Execution of Zulfikar Ali Bhutto
events.summary: Deposed in General Zia-ul-Haq's 1977 coup, former prime minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto is tried and hanged, ushering in a decade of military rule and Islamisation.

events.title: Pakistan conducts nuclear tests
events.summary: Following India's tests, Pakistan detonates nuclear devices in Balochistan, openly joining the ranks of nuclear-armed states.

events.title: Assassination of Benazir Bhutto
events.summary: Former prime minister Benazir Bhutto is assassinated at a rally weeks before national elections, after which civilian democratic rule is restored in 2008.

figures.name: Zulfikar Ali Bhutto
figures.role: President and Prime Minister of Pakistan
figures.blurb: A charismatic statesman who took power after the 1971 defeat, gave Pakistan its 1973 constitution and launched its nuclear programme, before being overthrown by Zia-ul-Haq and executed in 1979.

figures.name: Mohammad Zia-ul-Haq
figures.blurb: The army chief who seized power in 1977, had Bhutto hanged, and ruled through martial law and a programme of Islamisation until his death in a 1988 plane crash.

figures.name: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
figures.blurb: Quaid-e-Azam, the barrister who led the Muslim League and the movement that created Pakistan.

figures.name: Liaquat Ali Khan
figures.blurb: Jinnah's closest lieutenant, who led the new state until his assassination in 1951.

figures.name: Zulfikar Ali Bhutto
figures.blurb: Architect of the 1973 constitution and the nuclear programme, deposed and executed under Zia-ul-Haq.

sources.label: Pakistan: A Country Study
